rockies.title:
Mountain system in Canada

The Canadian Rockies mountain range spans the provinces of British Columbia and Alberta. With jagged, ice-capped peaks, including 3,954m Mt. Robson, it's a region of dramatic wilderness, alpine lakes, diverse wildlife and many outdoor recreation sites. National parks include Yoho, home to Takakkaw waterfall, dropping a sheer 302m, Jasper, famed for its Athabasca Glacier, and Banff, site of glacier-fed Lake Louise.

mississippi.title:
Mississippi River - New Orleans (Louisiana)
self made PRA

Algiers Point is seen at right. Across the River is the East Bank of New Orleans. At far left is the lower edge of the French Quarter with the Old Mint building visible. Going down river to the right, are the Marigny and Bywater neighborhoods of New Orleans, with the Naval Support Facility beside the Industrial Canal entrance being the large complex of buildings just across the river at the right edge of the photo. Parts of the Industrial Canal can be seen perpendicular to the River, with the Mississippi River-Gulf Outlet Canal paralleling the river to the top of the photo. In the distance at top left, Lake Pontchartrain.

colorado-river.title:
59026. Citadel Walls, Canon of the Grand, Utah. Photochrom print by the Detroit Photographic Co., copyrighted 1900.
The original title of this postcard is "Citadel Walls, Canyon of the Grand, Colorado River, Utah", but it seems this is Ruby Canyon, just inside Colorado. The Grand Canyon is in Arizona, not Utah, so the "Canyon of the Grand" must refer to something else. It turns out that the Colorado River, between its source and its confluence with the Green River, used to be called the Grand River, so this seem to be the Canyon of the Grand [River]. The railroad that follows the Colorado river (mostly on the north shore, so we're likely looking east) is the Denver & Rio Grande Railroad (map), which turns off just west of the Colorado-Utah border. So we're down to a very short stretch of the river and have to look for a location where the railroad tracks are wedged in between river and rock, with a distinctive peak between two valleys. If this particular rock formation is called Citadel Walls, I don't know. 
Today, the line is part of the Amtrak California Zephyr line, which means I've seen this place before.
From the Photochrom Prints Collection at the Library of Congress
More photochroms from Colorado & the United States | More photochrom prints

[PD] This picture is in the public domain

great-lakes.title: Denuded of all but the hardiest vegetation, the rugged, rocky shoreline of Lake Superior near the mouth of Horseshoe Bay takes on a desolate, almost lunar appearance, as seen from the Southern Headland Trail at Pukaskwa National Park, Ontario, Canada on a July 2015 afternoon. The barely discernible trail is marked by stone stairways, boardwalks, or bridges such as the one seen at right here, which help hikers not only to safely cross over crevices and other uneven terrain but also to determine what direction to go next.
great-lakes.author: Andre Carrotflower

great-lakes.title: This film photograph showcases the natural beauty of Pictured Rocks National Lakeshore as seen from the hiking trails that run alongside the lake's edge. The photo captures the contrast between the delicate birch trees in the foreground and the majestic sandstone cliffs in the background, with the roots of the trees visibly intertwined with the earth, emphasizing their struggle and resilience. The clear turquoise waters of Lake Superior and the arching rock formation known as Lovers Leap are visible in the distance, offering a glimpse into the geological history and natural splendor of Michigan's Upper Peninsula. The play of light and shadow, along with the natural framing of the scene, invites the viewer to appreciate the tranquility and rugged beauty of this unique shoreline.
great-lakes.author: Illeythekid

yellowstone.title:
Already in early October, Yellowstone is covered in snow and starting to shut down in portions for the year.

Yellowstone National Park sits on top of a geothermal hotspot that is thought to have been a massive supervolcano a million years ago. As a result, lots of geysers are found throughout the landscape. This is one. Normally they will billow steam like this, but occasionally they may erupt.
yellowstone.author: InSapphoWeTrust from Los Angeles, California, USA

yellowstone.title: Yellowstone National Park is home to diverse hot geyser pools.
yellowstone.author: Outdoorchronicle

yellowstone.title:
(1 in a multiple picture set)
There is so much hot water coming out of the ground in Norris Geyser Basin that streams of the very hot liquid flow from one to another.  Along these streams you see various colors which are the different bacteria living in the hot water.

It is a bit disconcerting to realize that when you walk through such a place, you are very close to the hot magma rising from the center of the earth.  In fact, you are as close as you will ever be to it.
yellowstone.author: inkknife_2000 (7.5 million views +)

yellowstone.title:
(1 in a multiple picture set)
When I saw how the bacteria in the water had turned this overflow to that color, I thought of molten gold.  The hot water flowing from the geysers contains thousands of species of bacteria, some new ones being discovered every day. When we see the word bacteria, we think of harmful organisms, but these are benign, and some have been helpful the research of medical solutions.
yellowstone.author: inkknife_2000 (7.5 million views +)

yellowstone-np.title: Mammoth Hot Springs terraces. Hot water is the creative force of the terraces.Even though Mammoth Hot Springs lie north of the caldera ring-fracture system, a fault trending north from Norris Geyser Basin, 21 miles (34 km) away, may connect Mammoth Hot Springs to the hot water of that system. A system of small fissures carries water upward to create approximately 50 hot springs in the Mammoth Hot Springs area. Another necessary ingredient for terrace growth is the mineral calcium carbonate. Thick layers of sedimentary limestone, deposited millions of years ago by vast seas, lie beneath the Mammoth area. As ground water seeps slowly downward and laterally, it comes in contact with hot gases charged with carbon dioxide rising from the magma chamber. Some carbon dioxide is readily dissolved in the hot water to form a weak carbonic acid solution. This hot, acidic solution dissolves great quantities of limestone as it works up through the rock layers to the surface hot springs. Once exposed to the open air, some of the carbon dioxide escapes from solution. As this happens, limestone can no longer remain in solution. A solid mineral reforms and is deposited as the travertine that forms the terraces.Dead trees in the terraces of Mammoth Hot Springs, Yellowstone National Park  grew during inactivity of the mineral-rich springs, and were killed when calcium carbonate carried by spring water clogged the vascular systems of the trees.
yellowstone-np.author: Brocken Inaglory
